title: JAMB English Language: Mastering Comprehension, Lexis, and Structure
author: Tklesson Team
content:
English Language is compulsory for every JAMB candidate. It is worth the same number of marks as your other three subjects combined in terms of its impact on your total score. A strong performance in English lifts your entire result. A weak performance drags everything down. Yet many candidates treat English as an afterthought, assuming that because they speak the language, they will do fine. The JAMB English paper is not a test of fluency. It is a test of specific skills: comprehension, vocabulary, grammar, and oral English. Here is how to prepare for each section and maximize your score.

The Structure of the JAMB English Paper

The JAMB English paper typically contains sixty questions drawn from several sections. Comprehension passages test your ability to read and understand written text. You will be given one or two passages followed by questions about the content, the author's intention, and the meaning of specific words or phrases in context. Lexis and structure questions test your vocabulary and your understanding of English grammar. You will be asked to choose the word or phrase that best completes a sentence, identify synonyms and antonyms, and apply grammatical rules. Oral English questions test your knowledge of pronunciation, stress patterns, intonation, and phonetic symbols.

Most candidates lose marks in lexis and structure and in oral English. These sections require specific knowledge that is not picked up through casual English use. You cannot absorb the rules of reported speech or the difference between a rising and falling intonation by chatting with friends. These topics must be studied deliberately.

How to Approach Comprehension Passages

Read the questions before you read the passage. This strategy transforms your reading from passive absorption into active search. When you know what the questions ask, you can identify the relevant sections of the passage as you read. Underline sentences that contain answers to the questions. This saves time when you are selecting your answers and reduces the chance of missing key information.

Answer based on the passage, not on your own opinion or general knowledge. JAMB comprehension questions test your understanding of what the author wrote, not what you think about the topic. If the passage states that exercise improves mental health, and a question asks about the author's view on exercise, the correct answer is the one that reflects what the passage says, even if you personally disagree or know of contrary evidence. Stay within the passage. It is the only source that matters for your answers.

Pay attention to vocabulary-in-context questions. JAMB often asks for the meaning of a word or phrase as it is used in the passage. The word may have multiple dictionary definitions, but only the one that fits the context is correct. Read the sentence containing the word. Read the sentences before and after it. The context tells you which meaning is intended. Do not rely on your prior knowledge of the word alone. Context determines meaning.

Mastering Lexis and Structure

The lexis and structure section tests your vocabulary and grammar. For vocabulary, you need to know synonyms, antonyms, and the appropriate word for a given context. The best way to prepare is to read widely and maintain a vocabulary notebook. When you encounter an unfamiliar word in your reading, write it down. Write the sentence in which you found it. Look up the definition. Write your own sentence using the word. The act of using the word in your own writing embeds it in your memory more effectively than memorizing definitions from a list.

For grammar, you need to know the rules. The most commonly tested topics include subject-verb agreement, tense consistency, reported speech, conditional sentences, prepositions, and phrasal verbs. These topics are rule-based. Once you understand the rule, you can apply it to any question. Use a grammar textbook or a reliable online resource to study each topic systematically. After studying a topic, practice with JAMB past questions. The repetition of applying the rule to actual exam questions solidifies your understanding.

Phrasal verbs are a particular challenge. A single verb can change meaning entirely depending on the preposition that follows it. To put off means to postpone. To put up with means to tolerate. To put across means to communicate. These meanings cannot be guessed from the individual words. They must be learned. Focus on the most commonly tested phrasal verbs in JAMB past questions. Do not try to memorize every phrasal verb in English. Prioritize the ones that appear repeatedly in past papers.

Preparing for Oral English

Oral English tests your knowledge of pronunciation, stress, intonation, and phonetic symbols. Many candidates neglect this section because it feels unfamiliar or because their schools did not teach it thoroughly. Neglecting oral English is a mistake. It carries a significant number of marks, and the questions are predictable once you understand the underlying concepts.

Learn the phonetic symbols. JAMB oral English questions often ask you to identify the vowel or consonant sound in a given word. You need to know the symbols for short vowels, long vowels, diphthongs, and consonants. Flashcards are effective for this. Write the symbol on one side and example words on the other. Practice until you can identify the sound in any word you encounter.

Understand stress patterns. English words have syllables, and one syllable is stressed, meaning it is pronounced with more emphasis than the others. JAMB asks you to identify the stressed syllable in a given word. The rules for stress placement are complex, but certain patterns are common. Two-syllable nouns often stress the first syllable. Two-syllable verbs often stress the second syllable. Words ending in -tion stress the syllable before the -tion. Practice with past questions to internalize these patterns.

The Bottom Line

JAMB English Language requires deliberate preparation, not passive reliance on everyday English skills. For comprehension, read the questions first and answer based on the passage alone. For lexis and structure, study vocabulary through reading and grammar through systematic topic review with past question practice. For oral English, learn phonetic symbols, stress patterns, and intonation rules. The English paper is worth the same as each of your other subjects. Give it the attention it deserves, and it will lift your total score significantly.
